slides.title: Why caching exists
body: Caching trades memory for speed. Save the result of a slow operation; the next caller gets it instantly instead of redoing the work.
Real example: Twitter's home timeline — your feed is computed once and served from cache to the millions of times you scroll. Recomputing per scroll would melt their servers.

vocabulary.def: A faster, more local store that holds results of expensive operations for reuse.
When the requested data is already in the cache and can be served fast.
When the requested data isn't cached, so you have to do the slow operation and save the result.

slides.title: TTL — time to live
body: Every cache entry has an expiration. After TTL elapses, the entry is invalid; the next reader does the slow work and refreshes the cache.
Real example: your DNS resolver caches 'salesforce.com → 13.x.x.x' for ~5 minutes, so every webpage doesn't re-query DNS.

vocabulary.def: Time To Live — how long a cache entry remains valid before it expires.

slides.title: Cache invalidation — the hard part
body: There are only two hard problems in computer science: cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-one errors. (Old joke. Mostly true.)
When the underlying data changes, stale cache entries need to be cleared — or you'll serve old data. Get this wrong and you'll have very confused users.

vocabulary.def: Removing or refreshing a cache entry when the underlying data changes.

slides.title: Cache-aside pattern
body: Most common pattern: check cache first → on miss, do the slow work → store the result in cache → return it.
Real example: every well-built web app uses cache-aside for database reads. Redis sits in front of Postgres; the app checks Redis first.

slides.title: Apex's static-variable cache
body: Apex static variables persist for the duration of ONE transaction. Anywhere in the same transaction, reads come from the static — no second SOQL.
Real example: a getCurrentUser() utility that queries User on first call, stores in a static, and returns the static on subsequent calls within the same trigger fire.

vocabulary.def: Cache that lives only for the current Apex transaction; reset between transactions.

slides.title: A simple static-variable cache
slides.intro: Type this. The User is queried at most once per transaction.
lineExplanations: Static field — persists across calls within a transaction.

slides.title: Salesforce Platform Cache
body: Persists ACROSS transactions, in-memory, with TTL. Two flavors: Org Cache (shared by all users) and Session Cache (per-user). Pre-allocate partitions in Setup.
Real example: a getCountryList() utility that pulls 250 countries from a custom object. Cache once for the whole org; every user-facing page hits cache after the first.

vocabulary.def: Salesforce's cross-transaction in-memory cache. Org Cache (shared) or Session Cache (per-user).

slides.title: Reading and writing Platform Cache
slides.intro: Type this. The 'apexCache' partition must already exist in Setup.
lineExplanations: Get a handle on the partition. 'local.' is the namespace prefix for non-managed code.
Read by key. Cast back to original type. Returns null on miss.
Real code would compute or query. Assign to the variable.
Store with a TTL of 3600 seconds (1 hour). After that, it expires.

slides.title: @AuraEnabled(cacheable=true)
body: For methods called from Lightning Web Components. cacheable=true tells Lightning 'safe to cache this on the client'. Same input → same output, served from browser memory.
Real example: a list of US states. Identical for every page render — perfect to cache in the browser. Only true read-only methods should be cacheable.

vocabulary.def: Annotation that lets LWC cache the method's result on the client. Read-only methods only.

slides.title: A cacheable LWC method
slides.intro: Type this. The framework caches the response in the browser per-input.
lineExplanations: @AuraEnabled(cacheable=true) marks this method as safe to cache on the client. Lightning components calling it get instant repeated reads.
Method must be public static and return only serializable types.
Body returns a list. In real code, this would query Salesforce.

slides.title: Custom Metadata as a config cache
body: Custom Metadata Types are deployable, queryable like SObjects, but cached by the platform — reads are essentially free, no SOQL counted.
Real example: feature flags, tax rates by country, integration settings — anything that changes rarely but is read often. Stripe-rate-by-country is a perfect Custom Metadata use case.

vocabulary.def: Salesforce-managed configuration records. Free to read (no SOQL governor cost), deployable, version-controlled.

slides.title: What should NEVER be cached
body: Anything that changes per-request: timestamps, pricing during checkout, security tokens, balances during a transfer, anything affecting a downstream WRITE.
Real example: bank balances. If you cache a balance and authorize a withdrawal off the cached value, you can authorize twice on a balance you don't have. This caused real bank failures.

slides.front: What's the simplest cache available in Apex, and how long does it live?
slides.back: A 'static' class variable. Lives for the duration of one transaction.
slides.detail: Cheap, no setup, perfect for 'don't query the same User twice within this trigger fire.' For longer-lived caches, use Platform Cache.

slides.question: You have a method getTaxRate(state) called from many LWC pages. Tax rates change once a year. What annotation?
options: @AuraEnabled — without cacheable, hits the server every time.
slides.explanation: @AuraEnabled(cacheable=true). Read-only, deterministic per input — perfect for client-side caching.

slides.title: What you now know
body: Cache fundamentals (TTL, invalidation, cache-aside), Apex's transaction-scoped static cache, Platform Cache (Org/Session), @AuraEnabled(cacheable=true), Custom Metadata as config cache.
Next: the Capstone — bringing every concept from Lessons 0-15 together on a real-world consulting scenario.
